posts.name: Takao Shimizu
posts.cooked:
I’m still in the journey of finding white wine I really like, but what I recently noticed is that what I look for the most in white wine is structure, and when I say it, it’s not just high acidity, but a phenolic grip or tannins on the palate. A wine that drinks almost like a red wine (ofc figuratively) if that makes sense.

If my description was unclear, I believe this quality is often described “chiseled”, “minerally”, “textural”, “steely” or “having dry extract” in a tasting note in a poetic way, but they probably don’t mean what I mean all the time, as people use “minerally” or other descriptors to say myriad of different things (e.g. I’m not talking about reductive character in its aroma).

Lamy-Caillat is the most extreme example of what I’m describing but I’ve found this quality in some other wine to a (much) lesser extent: Raveneau, Pierre Morey and Guiberteau. Is there any producers that comes to your mind for this quality? Ideally I’m hoping to find another wine that displays as much phenolic texture on the palate as Lamy-Caillat does, at much lower price. It doesn’t need to be a Burgundy and aromatic profile can be totally different.

As far as I understand, this quality comes from a harsh pressing of grapes and maybe exposure to solids during elevage, but this is just speculation based on what I’ve heard and experienced, and I’m not sure if this is the right way to track down the wines in the style I look for.

posts.name: Mikael B E K E L E
posts.cooked:
Think I know what you mean, a white burgundy opened my eyes to white wines.

One excellent option is dry riesling!

Most whites with a couple of days of maceration (or longer if you like amber wines).

posts.name: Rahsaan Maxwell
posts.cooked:
TakaoS:

Any specific producers or wine you suggest? I haven’t had ton of Riesling, but I haven’t encountered any Riesling that has grippy texture.

Riesling that spends a lot of time on the lees (Koehler-Ruprecht Auslese trockens) will show plenty of grippy chewy character. But while grapes vary in their structure, you can find this style in any region and from any grape, just depending on the specifics of the cuvee. Maybe ask your favorite retailer for a smattering across countries/regions.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
Mikael_OB:

One excellent option is dry riesling!

Typically German Riesling is just cold-soaked with the skins, which makes more aromatic compounds soluble while minimizing phenolic extraction. Phenolic extraction occurs during skin maceration while the wine is fermenting, as higher temperatures and alcohol help extracting phenolic compounds. However, I’ve understood that true skin maceration over fermentation is not that common.

Rahsaan_M:

Riesling that spends a lot of time on the lees (Koehler-Ruprecht Auslese trockens) will show plenty of grippy chewy character.

How? To my understanding, if you age on fine lees, you’ll get just creaminess and richer texture from the mannoproteins that come from autolysis, ie. when the yeast cells that make up the fine lees break up. Phenolic compounds are found in the gross lees and typically most wines are racked off the gross lees once the fermentation is over. You might get a richer texture and more leesy flavors, but not that phenolic, chewy and tannic grip from aging on fine lees.

Typically the normal way for a wine to get more phenolic content and higher dry extract is either through maceration over fermentation, less delicate crush and higher inclusion of press wine. Conversely, most modern whites are made by careful, delicate crush; no skin maceration; and small to no inclusion of press wine. I’ve understood that cold soak and prolonged aging on the lees (both of which are common winemaking practices) have quite a tiny impact on the phenolic content of the wine.

reply_to_user.name: Mikael B E K E L E

Phenolic compounds are found in the gross lees and typically most wines are racked off the gross lees once the fermentation is over. You might get a richer texture and more leesy flavors, but not that phenolic, chewy and tannic grip from aging on fine lees.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
Marcu_Stanley:

I’ve never had a white wine ever that had tannins in the sense that I understand red wines as having them. Never had a white wine dry out my mouth like that

I’ve had, but to the extent of how a wine like a Jura Poulsard grips, ie. very little. But some white wines do have a little bit of tannic grip. However, it’s something that is barely perceptible, meaning that you really have to look for it to actually notice it - it’s not anything even remotely like how a wine like a Bordeaux or a Nebbiolo can dry out your mouth.

IIRC Albariño has thick skins with a propensity to produce quite phenolic white wines if the grapes are not handled carefully, but typically the phenolic character results in pronounced bitterness in the taste and somewhat more textural mouthfeel, but I can’t remember if I’ve ever tasted an Albariño that had any perceptible tannic grip (apart from those made with extended skin maceration).

To be honest Otto, I to some extent disregarded the phenolic comment except suggesting to drink amber wines and those with a day or two of maceration.

The reason why I recommend Riesling was that later on saw the wines OP gave as example of liking:

“Lamy-Caillat is the most extreme example of what I’m describing but I’ve found this quality in some other wine to a (much) lesser extent: Raveneau, Pierre Morey and Guiberteau. Is there any producers that comes to your mind for this quality? Ideally I’m hoping to find another wine that displays as much phenolic texture on the palate as Lamy-Caillat does, at much lower price.”

I haven’t tried any of these producers white wines but i don’t think they are famous for skin maceration (?), hence my conclusion was that what he might actually appreciate and like is the acidic structure - that’s why I recommend Riesling!

@TakaoS - If we are thinking of the same thing in the end then you have tons of Riesling options, definitely doesn’t have to be GGs, you can sometimes find those qualities in great entry level rieslings. I find the acidic structure in anything dry/trocken from kabinett and upward. Especially Mosel but I only mention that as I drink quite a bit of it myself, but Rheinhessen producers like mentioned Keller, or Wittmann and Seehof are other good examples.

Mosel producers that have somehow in mind of making Mosel wines in the traditionally dry direction is a good option. Follow the Riesling thread and keep any eye out out for these wines (Falkenstein/Stein among these)!

posts.name: Wil Raley
posts.cooked:
Paul Pillot for my palate. QPR wise the Chassagne Village Les Mazures is really great. Stepping up to La Romanee is really something special, but you’re close to that Lamy Caillat price at that point.

You’re searching for white producers that crush their grapes and do long press cycles with the skins to absorb some phenolics. Then aged two winters on the lees. Generally 12 months on oak then racked with lees to stainless for another winter. Most producers that follow this style only do 6 months In stainless, but Lamy Caillat is notable for doing a full 12 months in stainless, so 24 months total on the lees - this really amps up the texture.

Walter Scott and Morgen Long are both worth seeking out. Both producers crush and have that phenolic structure to their wines. It’s worth pointing out though that this structure gets significantly more nuanced and pleasurable with a bit of bottle age, so don’t expect to snag a 23 Walter Scott and have the Lamy-Caillat experience. A 2019 WS X Novo might get you closer though.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
Mikael_OB:

To be honest Otto, I to some extent disregarded the phenolic comment except suggesting to drink amber wines and those with a day or two of maceration.

And I understood that exactly this phenolic thing was what OP was looking for - not like red wine-grippy orange wines with full-on tannins, but instead white wines that have the tiniest bit of tannic grip, resulting from winemaking choices. That’s a completely different thing from a) tannic orange wines; b) white wines with rich, soft texture from prolonged aging on the lees, or the combination of modest acidity and full body (like Viognier and the likes).

Mikael_OB:

I haven’t tried any of these producers white wines but i don’t think they are famous for skin maceration (?), hence my conclusion was that what he might actually appreciate and like is the acidic structure - that’s why I recommend Riesling!

Indeed you are correct that they basically never do any skin maceration in Burgundy - I’m not sure if they even do any cold-soak with the whites (with reds it can be common). However, if you crush and press your grapes more aggressively and add a good deal of press juice, you are going to get quite a bit of grape phenolics. That’s why many producers traditionally employed the technique of must browning - many of these phenolics have a propensity to oxidize. By letting the must oxidize and turn brown, you are going to lose some of the fruit, but also all the oxidized phenolic compounds are left behind when they drop out of the solution and only the non-oxidized phenolics now remain in the clear juice, contributing to the texture, structure and ageability of the resulting wine. Add in some wood phenolics from any new oak barrels and you’re having a white that has a considerably higher proportion of phenolic content than any Riesling that been cold-soaked for a day. It’s not about acidity (although higher acidity does accentuate the perception of tannins!) but the actual phenolics. You can have white Burgundies (and other similarly made wines) that might not have that much acidity, yet have a perceptible level of phenolic compounds that contribute to the overall taste, texture and structure of the wine.

I’ve seen @William_Kelley talk about the dry extract and phenolic content of not only white Burgs, but also Champagnes and how in the past the amount of phenolics and dry extract was much higher in these wines, resulting in wines that were much more tactile and ageworthy. I’m sure WK knows a thing or two how these things work, so I hope he can chime in!

Andrew_K:

These wines are very different from Riesling with nice acid. They have special texture due to pressing and elevage techniques

Correct.

Andrew_K:

But they are indeed special for a reason and unfortunately for the OP there is no “similar but cheaper” option.

Incorrect. Why do you always have to bring in the price element, even when it doesn’t have anything to do with the topic?

Brian_G_r_a_f_s_t_r_o_m:

The 2014 Trimbach Riesling Frédéric Emile is so dry it comes across as a bit tannic.

I think this is a completely different phenomenon altogether. I do agree that CFE is a wonderful wine, but I’ve never noticed it would be particularly phenolic in character.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
Mikael_OB:

I have admittedly (perhaps incorrectly) included the assumption that the OP might not yet know 100% what nuances that highlights what in the white wines he likes. If you’re in the beginning to working out what you like, one needs to explore and probably have your assumptions challenged.

Indeed, this is definitely always a possibility. I’ve noticed more than once that people at first seem to really know what they are talking about with all their technical details and whatnot. After you follow them a little bit more, you realize that they actually don’t seem to have any idea about what they are talking about. Such are internet forums and people talking there!

Anyways, high acid whites and Rieslings especially are always a good suggestion (no matter what somebody asked for)! Especially if the person asking for recommendations doesn’t know much about the subject.

However, if someone actually knows what they are talking about and is asking recommendations for whites with high phenolic content, blanket suggestions like Riesling, dry Sherry or Roussanne-Marsanne are not particularly helpful.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: I think it’s not quite so easy to say it’s just winemaking… old vine Loire Chenin is definitely inherently more phenolic than much Burgundy Chardonnay, so if you’re looking for wines with some structural grip then, irrespective of practices, the odds are weighted in your favor. Of course, that assumes that all things equal, and of course they very seldom are (a lot of Chenin is badly farmed, badly pressed, and left with RS). But I do think it makes sense you would like Guiberteau.

posts.cooked:
There are many amazing, mineral Albariños from Rías Baixas, definitely well wort checking out. Ditto to some of the best Vinho Verdes - some Portuguese Alvarinhos can be a bit more weighty and tropical compared to the leaner style of Rías Baixas Albariños, but the best Vinho Verde Alvarinhos can match the freshness and verve.

It’s hard to say whether you will find the phenolic character you were looking for in these wines, but they are still thrilling wines all the same.

posts.name: Rhoads Wilson
posts.cooked: Are there other Chenin producers in the Loire that stand out to you for their grip? Belargus immediately comes to my mind

posts.name: H 3n ry  Br 1m 0
posts.cooked: Dry Furmint would be worth seeking out. I find the majority of those wines that I taste have a perceptible phenolic grip character, plus they are affordable!

posts.name: Doug Sher
posts.cooked: Some Josko Gravner whites have come across as very tannic, if I remember correctly. Its been a few years since ive had one.

posts.name: patrick c albright
posts.cooked: Ganevat and Labet.

posts.name: Takao Shimizu
posts.cooked:
I only found one tasting note for dry Furmint from a long time ago, but looking at it, I indeed wrote down it had a phenolic grip.

That said, it was a cheap example readily available in the US, and I also noted overall quality was not very high - lacking fruit intensity and freshness, with heat showing and being a bit flat on the palate.

Do you have a fine example or two you’d recommend for the region?

posts.name: Takao Shimizu
posts.cooked: Is that not a skin contact wine? I’m not too familiar with the producer, so maybe they make a classic white wine with a phenolic grip too, but I thought their wines are generally bordering to be an orange wine (even if they categorize it as a white wine).

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Boudignon!

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Furmint does indeed have thick, quite tannic skins.

So does Sémillon…

It’s no accident that these cépages are used to make sweet wines, as the bitterness from the skins balances the residual sugar.

posts.name: Eric Ifune
posts.cooked: White Rioja and Portuguese wines such as Perimanca Branco. Also some white Douro table wines.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
Bordering? Josko Gravner makes probably the best orange wines in Italy - if not in the whole world - so if they are not orange wines, I don’t know what are!

They might be called “white wines” but Gravner “whites” are as orange wines as wines come. And yes, they are most definitely tannic and very phenolic in all kinds of ways.

posts.name: Tom Wilcox
posts.cooked: Some Châteauneuf-du-Pape blanc definitely has this character

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
TakaoS:

Do you have a fine example or two you’d recommend for the region?

If you have access to any Hungarian dry whites, I think Szepsy is probably the single best producer there. Both dry and sweet whites are exceptional. Often quite pricey, too.

Lenkey, Attila Homonna and Zoltán Demeter are some other excellent producers. Truly singular world-class wines.

Fekete used to be the greatest producer im Somló, but since the old man Bela sold his winery to a few younger guys and retired, I don’t know how the things are, because I really haven’t really tasted these younger vintages.

Outside Hungary, Verus (Slovenia) and Bott Frigyes (Slovakia) make some very noteworthy wines as well.

posts.name: H 3n ry  Br 1m 0
posts.cooked: Oremus Tokaji Furmint Mandolas is widely avaiable in US and should be around or under $30USD.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
Henry_Br:

Oremus Tokaji Furmint Mandolas is widely avaiable in US and should be around or under $30USD.

While a decent wine in its own right, I don’t think this is particularly representative of the dry Tokaji white style - it feels more like a Burgundian take of Furmint than classic dry Tokaji (if there even is a “classic” style, as dry Tokaji as a style is a rather modern invention) - nor that Oremus is at the same level of quality as the Hungarian producers I mentioned when it comes to dry whites.

But, doesn’t hurt to check the wine out. I’ve seen that Mandolas actually ages quite nicely, so the wine might be a bit better with a few years under its belt than immediately after release - I’ve found the younger bottles a bit too woody.

posts.name: Rauno E N G el
posts.cooked: If it’s the textural, grippy quality you’re after, I would also suggest trying some of the high quality Assyrtiko wines (e.g. single vineyard ones from Santorini).

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
Rauno_E_NZ:

If it’s the textural, grippy quality you’re after, I would also suggest trying some of the high quality Assyrtiko wines (e.g. single vineyard ones from Santorini).

Now that’s a great suggestion and I wonder why on earth I didn’t think of that earlier, since I’ve always thought the Santorini Assyrtikos have a very saline, phenolic and almost grainy, mineral texture - and we’ve just had one Assyrtiko tasting a few weeks ago and we’re having another in early May!

But indeed, it’s basically the Santorini Assyrtikos you want to check. Assyrtikos from mainland Greece have never left a particularly memorable impression on me and Assyrtikos from the other islands tend to be more about richness and fruit, whereas the Santorini Assyrtikos are very singular in their intensely non-fruity overall character. Argyros, Artemis Karamolegos and Hatzidakis are names one should really check out.

posts.name: H 3n ry  Br 1m 0
posts.cooked: Sometimes the best ability is availability!

posts.name: Takao Shimizu
posts.cooked: Unfortunately they don’t seem available where I buy, but maybe one day in Europe

posts.name: Takao Shimizu
posts.cooked: I might be able to find this one - will check!

posts.name: Takao Shimizu
posts.cooked: Not a lot of experiences with Assyrtiko except for entry level ones - will check them out!

posts.name: Takao Shimizu
posts.cooked:
Semillon might be another good suggestion - I tend to like neutral varieties much better than aromatic ones.

I’m not experienced with Semillon either, but I noted a textural grip for some Hunter Valley Semillon, based on old tasting notes. It seems I never found the same character for Bordeaux Blanc, but I probably haven’t tried enough to be qualified to say that.

Maybe early harvest in a subtropical climate leads to preservation of grippy texture in phenolics? I still don’t understand well how differently climate affects the phenolic ripeness and physiological ripeness.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I think there are a lot of variables…

What we are talking about here is the perception of phenolic “grip”, in other words, a sort of bitterness. For the perception of bitterness:

  • quality is more important than quantity: unripe phenolics, even if less abundant, are perceptibly bitterer
  • acidity amplifies the perception of bitterness
  • residual CO2 amplifies the perception of bitterness
  • high levels of free SO2 amplify the perception of bitterness

My perception, as far from an expert on the region, is that these four criteria are all often met by Hunter Valley Sémillon. Whether the analytically measurable levels of dry extract are all that high, however, is another matter.

White Burgundy made from concentrated fruit, crushed heavily and pressed firmly can easily have >20g/l dry extract (a crude proxy for what we are talking about, but at least a quantifiable one). Due to ripeness and barrel maturation on the lees, however, they need not taste “coarse” or crudely bitter/astringent. Rather, the bitterness is integrated into a harmonious whole and passes unperceived by many tasters.

posts.name: Takao Shimizu
posts.cooked:
Thanks for the insights! My brain could be well fooled into mixing the two, but I’ve always seen bitterness and astringency as two distinct perceptions - the former as a taste (binding to a taste bud) and the latter as a mechanical sensation (binding to proteins which causes changes in pressure). By this, I mean black coffee is more bitter than astringent while well made Barolo is more astringent than bitter.

I meant “a touch of astringency” when I wrote “phenolic grip”, and I suspect impact of other variables you mentioned are mostly for bitterness. All that said, your point that the quality of phenolics matters more than quantity makes a lot of sense.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
FWIW, I think most wine enthusiasts - including many people here on this board - consider these terms interchangeable, or they can’t even differentiate between these two perceptions.

I, however, am on the same boat as you. Bitterness is a very distinct thing from astringency - tannins can be bitter, astringent or both. However, a bitter taste is a very different thing from astringent grip, and this is a distinction I want to make in all my tasting notes in which I comment on either of them.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Ultimately it is the same set of compounds that is responsible for the taste and the sensation, so I think they are functionally equivalent for the purposes of this discussion…

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

Ultimately it is the same set of compounds that is responsible for the taste and the sensation, so I think they are functionally equivalent for the purposes of this discussion…

It’s true that for example grape tannins can be responsible for both the taste and the sensation, ie. tannins can taste bitter (shorter chain molecules), be noticed as astringent ie. grippy feeling on the gums (longer chains), or just textural, ie. not bitter nor grippy but they contribute to the dry extract, lending a more tactile texture to the wine (even longer chains). However, even if they are made up of same compounds, I’d say they are not functionally equivalent, since the degree of tannin polymerization can change considerably how a wine tastes / feels on the palate. I’ve had wines that are considerably tannic yet don’t taste one bit bitter; or have had wines that have had a very bitter taste, yet no noticeable tannic grip. In this sense it doesn’t make sense to say they are functionally equivalent, ie. a wine has noticeable tannic bitterness, if the wine doesn’t bitter at all but is considerably grippy.

Furthermore, tannins are just one part of the phenolics that can get into wine. There are tons of other phenolics that can contribute to the taste and/or the sensation, so it’s entirely possible that, for example, a wine tastes bitter because of some other phenolic compounds than tannins, so in this case it would also be technically incorrect to describe the wine as “tannic” if it just tastes bitter (although most likely a normal person couldn’t say why the wine tastes bitter without in-depth chemical analyses!).

So all in all, I’d argue that bitterness and astringency are not functionally equivalent, even if on the surface level both these qualities are caused by the same compounds on a larger level - phenolics.

posts.name: Tess Kazi
posts.cooked:
Such a thought stimulating conversation here.

I wonder what aging on lees relative to/ rather than malolactic fermentation contributes to phenolic character in whites. I’m thinking about some Chablis that might be described as ‘more rounded’ but to me are better described as having a nutty (though not buttery in the sense of white burgundy) textural quality to them. I’m also thinking about Chenin. But then Santorini Assyrtiko is also often aged on lees. I’m told long period lees aging also occurs with some Albarino (‘seleccion’) in addition to a bit of MLF.

posts.name: Ben M a n d l e r
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

Ultimately it is the same set of compounds that is responsible for the taste and the sensation, so I think they are functionally equivalent for the purposes of this discussion…

Bitterness is probably one of the least well understood aspects of wine’s sensory profile. In part I think this is because we have been operating under this assumption. I’ve worked on both bitterness and phenolic analysis of wines and I no longer believe this to be true, at least not in the way that people talk about.

To make matters worse, running sensory panels for bitterness and astringency specifically in white wines, it is apparent that people (a) have very different experiences of bitterness from one another, both in sensitivity and in the types of things they find bitter, and (b) their bitterness perception is not strongly related to their astringency perception except for the fact that their salivary flow is predictive of duration and peak intensity for both, all other things being equal (which they aren’t).

Without assigning chemical identities to the compounds at play, it is fair to say that astringency comes primarily from skins while bitterness comes primarily from seeds. So they’re probably not the same in either chemistry or in origin, which has implications for their management in the vineyard and winery. And none of this is to say that a little bitterness can’t be good, or can’t enhance perceptions of astringency, or isn’t in fact part of what some people are talking about when they talk about grip!
posts.display_username: Ben M a n d l e r

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: It doesn’t contribute it. Phenolics are coming from the skins. Tannins in white wines can bind with polysaccharides from the lees to become more stable and smoother.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
It doesn’t help that the French don’t use “amers” (bitters) the same way we would in English. Most of my talking about wine (as opposed to writing) occurs in French, and I have noticed myself using English words in a French sense, erroneously, on several occasions.

As we are talking about white wines, however, where seed extraction isn’t an issue, by your definition most of this thread should have been talking about astringency as opposed to bitterness.

But I do still maintain that, given that the distinction in this thread is between wines with the desired characteristics and those without them, and that those characteristics are mostly attributable to the degree of skin extraction during pressing, that, pace Otto, what we actually call those characteristics represents a tangent more than the crux of the matter.

reply_to_user.name: Ben M a n d l e r

posts.name: Tom DeBiase
posts.cooked:
Ben_M_a_n_d_l_e_r:

To make matters worse, running sensory panels for bitterness and astringency specifically in white wines, it is apparent that people (a) have very different experiences of bitterness from one another, both in sensitivity and in the types of things they find bitter, and (b) their bitterness perception is not strongly related to their astringency perception except for the fact that their salivary flow is predictive of duration and peak intensity for both, all other things being equal (which they aren’t).

To reach an appropriate conclusion you need a fully trained sensory panel with descriptive analysis and agreed upon standards and benchmarks.

Tom

reply_to_user.name: Ben M a n d l e r

posts.name: Ben M a n d l e r
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

It doesn’t help that the French don’t use “amers” (bitters) the same way we would in English. Most of my talking about wine (as opposed to writing) occurs in French, and I have noticed myself using English words in a French sense, erroneously, on several occasions.

Fascinating, these language differences. Would love to learn more about that some time. Not trying to derail the thread here. I think that how wine professionals talk about these sensory experiences is important - especially when we relate a sensory experience to quantifiable chemical compounds - because there’s a lot of misattribution and we’re talking across each other if we’re using the same language to mean different things. But I agree that in general the finer points of these distinctions are not going to materially impact our attempts to help the OP find what they are looking for.
posts.display_username: Ben M a n d l e r

posts.name: Rauno E N G el
posts.cooked: What would be some of the more rounded Chablis that display those characteristics in your view? In my experience, I would describe Pattes Loup in this way, but also Piuze (to a somewhat different extent).

posts.name: Takao Shimizu
posts.cooked:
I think William’s original point holds whether we’re talking about bitterness or astringency, but for my original question, such distinction matters 100% because I’m not asking for wine that is bitter.

One example that comes to my mind is an inexpensive Soave. A good number of Soave wine I’ve had displayed a subtle bitterness in aftertaste, but not any textural grip. Garganega seems like a thick-skinned variety, so maybe that comes from the skin phenolics. Maybe not. But either way, wine with a subtle bitterness has almost nothing to do with wine with a textural grip I’m looking for, at least in terms of sensory experience, even if both characteristics come from a same set of compounds.

Now I think about it, I suspect many Albariño wine might fall into this group (having bitterness) rather than being wine with a textural grip, perhaps along with some of the aromatic varieties that are not discussed here.

reply_to_user.name: Ben M a n d l e r

posts.name: Tess Kazi
posts.cooked: I’m not sure that I would call it rounded so much as ‘nutty’. I was thinking of a Droin from Vaudesir.

posts.name: Tess Kazi
posts.cooked: What about the ‘structure’ or minerality, beyond smoothness and ‘creaminess’, that sur lie aging is said to give? Some winemakers seem to employ extended time on lees but resist battonage (and new barrels) to add structure while limiting a wine’s unctuousness. Again, I’m thinking about Chablis.

posts.name: Paul Kaletsch
posts.cooked: What a fascinating thread. Thank you, even though I must say I’m a bit in over my head and would like to ask a clarifying question: I came to this because I drank a 14 abv sancerre last night that tasted distinctly bitter, though, not unpleasantly so and not lacking integration and balance. I referred to this as phenolic bitterness. On other occasions, I use dry extract in my white wine tasting notes, however. But essentially I mean the same: something bitter that doesn’t grip. So, what’s the correct descriptor for this?

posts.name: P a u l Christensen
posts.cooked:
This is indeed a fascinating thread - thanks to all the contributors.

As it happens as I type this I’m drinking a gargenega / sauvignon blanc blend which is raising all sorts of issues for me defining these aspects of bitterness, grip/drying mouthfeel, and even cat’s pee (is that bitter?). I guess the point of WSET and more advanced wine tasting learning is at least to be able to standardise our usage of these terms, but I still struggle often.

Also, as an aside to @William_Kelley 's point about French/English non-equivalence, I used to work for a major French multinational, and one thing I rapidly came to realise is that when a French person speaking English says “normally” they normally don’t mean normally as we use it in English - they are using it in the sense of "normalement: in French which (at least when used without directly qulifying a verb) is something like “in principle” in English.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
PaulK:

I referred to this as phenolic bitterness. On other occasions, I use dry extract in my white wine tasting notes, however. But essentially I mean the same: something bitter that doesn’t grip. So, what’s the correct descriptor for this?

It can be phenolic bitterness, but I think there are several other compounds that can also taste bitter, so unless you’re sure that the bitterness comes from phenolics, I think it’s just safe to say that there’s some bitterness in the taste.

Indeed, phenolic compounds can taste bitter, they can grip, they can do both and also neither. It depends on which phenolic compounds there are in the wine and its other chemical properties.

High dry extract can refer to a higher-than-normal level of phenolic compounds, but as said before, it doesn’t necessarily mean that the wine is bitter or grippy in any way. For example many white Burgundies high in dry extract might just have a very rich, almost chewy texture compared to a white Burgundy very low in dry extract, which might come across as very lightweight with an almost thin mouthfeel.

P_a_u_l_Christensen:

cat’s pee (is that bitter?)

Nope. Cat’s pee is a flavor. Bitterness is a taste. Flavors are volatile compounds you technically smell retronasally and your brain makes you think you taste the flavors in your mouth.
Taste is something you sense directly with your tongue. Normally (pun intended) the classic five tastes are sweet, salty, bitter, acidic, umami. These are elements your tongue’s receptor can perceive - they don’t detect any flavors, like Sauvignon Blanc’s cat’s pee.

posts.name: Paul Kaletsch
posts.cooked:
Thanks so much. This is very helpful, especially your distinction between taste and flavor. just to double check - how does this work re tasting notes? when someone mentions green apple, for instance, this implies acidic/sour + a certain flavor.

I think Jasper Morris once said that, even though it would be correct, no one really says that a wine tastes sour/acidic because it comes with a negative connotation. I’d say the same goes for bitterness.

When I read a tasting note, I get a first impression when someone writes bitter/acidic, but I want to know more about the acidity/bitterness both in terms of flavor and structure to assess if I like it or not. With acidity a few things come to mind: crisp/lively/racy/laser-sharp and so forth. I haven’t seen the use of bitterness much except in reference to tannins (and occasionally phenolic bitterness).

posts.name: Takao Shimizu
posts.cooked:
PaulK:

when someone mentions green apple, for instance, this implies acidic/sour + a certain flavor.

Typically not, but there’s no law regulating how each descriptor should be used, so we never know what author exactly experienced. As you ask this question, I suspect many people don’t pay attention to whether they’re describing taste or flavour by a certain descriptor. That said, I can think of several descriptors that are likely about both taste and flavour. For example, citrus pith is often flavour and subtle bitterness on the palate.

PaulK:

With acidity a few things come to mind: crisp/lively/racy/laser-sharp and so forth

Our taste bud measures the level of pH, meaning strictly speaking, there’s no texture in acidity. It’s one dimensional: high or low. What changes the perception of acidity is other sensory experiences that are perceived together. Most typically, perceived sweetness or fruity flavours often trick our brain into thinking the acidity is “soft”, “round” or “juicy”. Many times when someone has a problem with acidity in wine, it’s not the amount of acid in wine, but rather lack of sugar or flavour that makes the wine thin, harsh or unbalanced. This is for example why sushi chef put a tiny amount of sugar in sushi rice with vinegar.

PaulK:

I haven’t seen the use of bitterness much except in reference to tannins (and occasionally phenolic bitterness).

I think bitterness comes from some sort of phenolics or alcohol. Tannins are a type of phenolics, and of course unripe tannins are common cause of bitterness, but certain varieties even white ones often have bitterness without tannins. High abv often leads to a bitter aftertaste. Many people do not use these terms strictly, so I would personally take what’s in a random tasting note on CT with a grain of salt.
